text: Extended left hepatic lobectomy with chordate lobectomy on block for metastatic carcinoma to the liver is illustrated. CT scan shows a centrally situated tumor lying close to the vena cava which is indicated by the arrow. The tumor abuts the right side of the chordate process and obstructs the middle hepatic vein. This scan illustrates the position of the tumor invading and obstructing the middle hepatic vein. The left hepatic vein is indicated by the yellow arrow and the right hepatic vein by the blue arrow. Note that the tumor is closely applied to the right hepatic vein, but has somewhat greater clearance on the left side. The green arrow indicates an incidental hemangioma in the tip of the left lateral segment. The position of the tumor is again shown in this scan with close approximation to the right hepatic vein, suggesting that extended right hepatic lobectomy may be the appropriate operation. intraoperative ultrasound was carried out and illustrates a very close approximation of the tumor to the left portal pedicle as shown by the yellow arrow.

The surgeon exposes and dissects the left portal vein, isolating it before dividing it below the take-off of the caudate vein using an endogia vascular stapler. The left hepatic duct is encircled with a suture for later control. The line of demarcation separating liver segments two, three, and four from segments five and eight is visible and marked.
